title: Paphos Harbour Quarter tops out first phase four weeks early
excerpt: The final structural slab of the Harbour Quarter's residential terraces was poured this week, marking the topping-out of phase one ahead of programme.

content.text: At 06:40 on Tuesday morning, the last of 214 concrete pours on the Paphos Harbour Quarter's first phase was completed, the roof slab of the western residential terrace, seven storeys above the old fishing harbour. The traditional olive branch was craned to the summit in front of the full site crew, four weeks ahead of the contract programme.

The milestone caps fourteen months of structural work in one of the most constrained sites the group has taken on: a working harbour on one side, a protected archaeological zone on the other, and a single access road shared with the town's morning fish market. Deliveries were sequenced overnight and every crane lift plotted against harbour traffic.

Fit-out of the waterfront colonnade begins this month, with the public promenade, the project's gift to the town, on track to open to residents and visitors alike next summer. "The structure is the easy half," said site director Marios Ioannou. "Now we make it somewhere people will argue about who saw first."

coverImageAlt: Tower crane silhouetted against an amber evening sky

title: Characom awarded Larnaca coastal defence contract
excerpt: The group has signed a 34-month contract with the Republic for 3.2 kilometres of coastal protection and promenade works along Larnaca's eastern shore.

content.text: Characom Group has been named contractor for the Larnaca Coastal Defence Works following an open public tender, the group's largest marine contract to date. The 34-month programme covers 3.2 kilometres of shoreline east of the marina: submerged groynes, rock revetments, beach nourishment and a rebuilt public promenade above them.

The tender was evaluated on technical method as well as price. Characom's submission proposed placing the heaviest armour stone from a jack-up barge rather than the foreshore, halving truck movements through the tourist district during the summer seasons, a detail the evaluation committee singled out in its award notice.

Mobilisation begins immediately, with marine ecology surveys and stone sourcing from licensed Cypriot quarries already under way. It is the group's 85th public contract since 1999, and, as chairman Andreas Characos noted at the signing, "the first one where the site sets its own tide table."

coverImageAlt: Team reviewing documents around a meeting-room table

title: Group commits to 40% recycled aggregate on public works by 2028
excerpt: A new sustainability programme sets binding targets for recycled materials, on-site solar power and zero inert waste to landfill across all Characom sites.

content.text: Characom Group today published its 2026–2028 sustainability programme, committing to source at least 40% of aggregate on public works from certified recycled demolition material by 2028, up from 18% today. The target applies to every government contract the group tenders from this year forward.

The programme also brings the group's batching plants and site offices onto renewable power. A 1.2 MW solar installation at the Nicosia precast yard enters service this autumn, expected to cover the yard's full daytime demand, while all new site compounds will be specified with photovoltaic canopies as standard.

"Concrete is the most honest material there is, it shows you exactly what you put into it," said director of engineering quality Katerina Georgiou. "The same should be true of a construction group. These are numbers our clients can audit, not adjectives." Progress will be reported annually alongside the group's ISO 14001 surveillance audits.

coverImageAlt: Rows of solar panels under a bright sky

title: Characom launches paid apprenticeship programme with technical schools
excerpt: Twenty paid placements a year will bring students from Cyprus's technical schools onto live Characom sites, with a guaranteed interview at graduation.

content.text: Characom Group has signed agreements with technical schools in Nicosia, Limassol and Larnaca to launch a structured apprenticeship programme, offering twenty paid placements each academic year across the group's sites and its Nicosia precast yard. Apprentices rotate through setting-out, formwork, steel-fixing and site management over nine months.

Every apprentice is paired with a tradesperson mentor with at least fifteen years on the tools, and completes the same certified safety training as full-time staff. Graduates of the programme receive a guaranteed interview, and the group expects to hire the majority into its 650-strong workforce.

The programme responds to a shortage the whole industry talks about but rarely funds: the next generation of Cypriot builders. "We were founded by people who learned this trade with their hands," said CEO Elena Characou. "If we want craft to outlast us, we have to teach it, on real sites, for real pay."

coverImageAlt: Group of colleagues joining hands over a work table
